items.q: What is an iterative design process?
items.correct: A cyclical process where designs are repeatedly tested, evaluated, and refined.
items.misconception: A linear process from brief to final product.
items.random_wrong: A process used only for software.
items.why: Students learn a 'step 1, step 2' sequence and apply it to iteration too. Iteration loops back - you might redesign after testing, then test again, many times.

items.q: What is the difference between ductility and malleability?
items.correct: Ductility is being drawn into wires; malleability is being hammered into sheets.
items.misconception: They mean exactly the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Ductility is for metals, malleability is for plastics.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms because both involve deformation. Ductility = tensile (stretching into wires); malleability = compressive (rolling/hammering into sheets). Some metals excel at one but not the other.

items.q: What is FEA used for in product design?
items.correct: Simulating how a product will respond to forces, heat, or vibration before manufacture.
items.misconception: Final exam analysis of student designs.
items.random_wrong: Counting the elements in a chemical compound.
items.why: Students unfamiliar with the acronym guess at it. FEA = Finite Element Analysis: dividing a part into many small elements and simulating physical behaviour computationally - saves prototyping costs.

items.q: Whose 2005 Double Diamond models the design process?
items.why: It's UK Design Council. “IDEO” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which technique systematically alters product attributes?
items.why: Correct answer: SCAMPER. “TRIZ” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many diamonds are in the Double Diamond model?
items.why: The real figure is 2 diamonds. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
